past_medical_history: Iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago, on oral supplementation
Migraine without aura, occasional episodes since adolescence
family_history: Mother with breast cancer diagnosed at age 48, treated with surgery and chemotherapy
No family history of neurological disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.exercise: Recreational volleyball player, recently had to stop due to coordination difficulties

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cooperative. Appears uncomfortable and nauseated. Speech fluent without dysarthria. No aphasia. Attention and short-term memory grossly intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Bilateral papilledema on fundoscopic examination with blurred disc margins and venous engorgement. Extraocular movements intact but with gaze-evoked nystagmus on rightward gaze. Facial sensation and strength symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities. No pronator drift. Normal bulk and tone bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, and proprioception intact throughout. No sensory level identified.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps, triceps, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Bilateral dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, worse on the right. Dysdiadochokinesia bilaterally, more prominent on the right. Heel-to-shin testing impaired on the right.
neurological_exam.gait: Wide-based, unsteady gait with tendency to veer to the right. Unable to perform tandem gait. Romberg test positive with eyes open and closed.
neurological_exam.additional: Positive bilateral papilledema concerning for elevated intracranial pressure. No meningismus. No Brudzinski or Kernig signs.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headaches, nausea, vomiting, and worsening coordination over two months.
patient.history_present_illness: This 21-year-old right-handed Hispanic woman presents with a two-month history of progressively worsening headaches, nausea, and vomiting. The headaches began insidiously approximately eight weeks ago, initially described as a dull, occipital pressure that she attributed to stress from her college coursework. Over the past month, the headaches have become more severe and persistent, now occurring daily, often waking her from sleep in the early morning hours. They are associated with nausea and non-bilious vomiting, which has worsened over the past two weeks to the point where she has difficulty keeping food down and has lost approximately 4 kg of body weight. She reports that her typical migraines feel different from these new headaches, noting the current ones are more diffuse, worse with Valsalva maneuvers, and do not respond to her usual sumatriptan. She also describes progressive difficulty with balance and coordination over the past six weeks. She first noticed clumsiness when handling objects and dropping her phone more frequently. Over the past month, she has had increasing difficulty walking, describing a sense of unsteadiness and veering to the right side. She had to stop playing recreational volleyball three weeks ago because she could no longer coordinate her movements effectively. Her roommate noted that her handwriting has become progressively more illegible. She denies any seizures, loss of consciousness, visual changes other than occasional blurred vision, hearing loss, weakness, numbness, or tingling. She has not had fevers, night sweats, weight loss beyond what she attributes to poor oral intake, or any recent infections. She has no history of trauma. Her family history is notable for breast and ovarian cancer on the maternal side, which has caused her significant anxiety.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive
background.overall_organization: Mildly disorganized with intermittent generalized slowing

findings.location: Bilateral posterior regions, right greater than left

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, does not significantly obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient nausea and concern for elevated intracranial pressure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating intermittent generalized and posterior-predominant slowing, more prominent over the right hemisphere. No definite epileptiform discharges identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed. Recording duration 40 minutes, routine study.

findings.location: Right cerebellar hemisphere extending to the cerebellar vermis

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous signal intensity with areas of intrinsic T1 hyperintensity suggesting subacute hemorrhage
signal_characteristics.T2: Mixed signal intensity with areas of T2 hyperintensity and hypointensity consistent with blood products of varying ages
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the periphery of the enhancing component, suggesting high cellularity
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Irregular, heterogeneous ring enhancement with central non-enhancing areas consistent with necrosis

findings.mass_effect: Compression of the fourth ventricle with early obstructive hydrocephalus. Mild effacement of the cerebellopontine angle cistern on the right.
findings.borders: Irregularly contoured with poorly defined margins, infiltrative appearance

findings.location: Superior vermis, adjacent to and possibly contiguous with the right cerebellar mass

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Transependymal flow of CSF, periventricular FLAIR hyperintensity

findings.mass_effect: Mild dilation of lateral and third ventricles

additional_observations: No evidence of leptomeningeal enhancement or drop metastases along the visualized spinal canal
Mild perilesional edema surrounding the dominant right cerebellar mass
No evidence of supratentorial parenchymal lesions
Incidental 4 mm pineal cyst, benign in appearance
mri.impression: Two irregularly contoured, heterogeneously enhancing mass lesions involving the right cerebellar hemisphere and vermis with evidence of intratumoral hemorrhage, central necrosis, and surrounding edema. Compression of the fourth ventricle with early obstructive hydrocephalus and transependymal CSF flow. Imaging characteristics raise concern for a high-grade neoplasm. Given the posterior fossa location in a young patient, differential diagnosis includes primary high-grade glioma (glioblastoma), metastatic disease, ependymoma, medulloblastoma, and hemangioblastoma. The infiltrative margins and heterogeneous enhancement with necrosis favor a high-grade glial tumor. An incidental benign pineal cyst is also noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 34.1 % (L), MCV 76.5 fL (L), ESR 28 mm/hr (H), CRP 5.2 mg/L (H).

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the dominant right cerebellar mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV_ratio: 3.8 (compared to contralateral normal-appearing cerebellar white matter)
signal_characteristics.perfusion_pattern: Markedly elevated perfusion in the enhancing rim with low perfusion centrally consistent with necrosis

additional_observations: The elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim strongly favors a high-grade neoplasm over abscess or low-grade tumor
No elevated perfusion in the vermian component, which may represent less vascular or more necrotic tissue
output.impression: These perfusion characteristics strongly favor high-grade glioma over brain abscess, metastasis, or hemangioblastoma. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right cerebellar mass, single voxel and multivoxel

additional_observations: Reduced NAA reflects neuronal loss or displacement
No amino acid peaks that would suggest abscess
output.impression: No findings to suggest abscess (no amino acid peaks) or demyelinating disease. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT_Staging.value: No pulmonary nodules or masses. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal.
Liver, spleen, kidneys, adrenals unremarkable. No suspicious masses or lymphadenopathy. Small physiologic ovarian cyst on the left, 2.1 cm. No ascites.

Histopathology.value: Hypercellular glial tumor with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Histological Diagnosis Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO Grade 4 report (H), Cellular Features Hypercellular glial tumor with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis report (H), Ki-67 Proliferation Index 35% % (H), IDH1/IDH2 Mutation Wildtype (negative) report (H), MGMT Promoter Methylation Methylated report (H), TERT Promoter Mutation Present report (H), EGFR Amplification Present report (H).
abnormal_values_summary: MGMT promoter methylated (favorable for temozolomide response)

signal_characteristics.motor_cortex: Primary motor cortex activation identified bilaterally. No overlap between tumor and primary motor areas.
signal_characteristics.language_areas: Not applicable — posterior fossa location is remote from cortical language areas
signal_characteristics.cerebellar_function: Right cerebellar activation during motor tasks is absent in the region of the tumor. Compensatory left cerebellar activation observed.

additional_observations: Tumor does not involve the brainstem directly, though there is mass effect on the dorsal pons
Surgical corridor to the right cerebellar mass via suboccipital craniotomy appears feasible
DTI tractography shows displacement of the superior cerebellar peduncle but no frank invasion
output.impression: Pre-surgical functional MRI demonstrates that the right cerebellar mass does not directly involve primary cortical motor or language areas. However, the tumor has disrupted right cerebellar motor network function with compensatory contralateral cerebellar activation. DTI shows displacement but not invasion of the superior cerebellar peduncle. A suboccipital surgical approach is feasible. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cerebellar glioblastoma young adult prognosis treatment

results.title: Cerebellar glioblastoma: a systematic review and pooled survival analysis
results.summary: Cerebellar GBMs represent 0.4-3.4% of all GBMs. Median age at diagnosis is 50-55 years but can occur in younger patients. Median overall survival is 11-14 months with standard treatment. Cerebellar GBM may have slightly better prognosis than supratentorial GBM when gross total resection is achieved.

results.title: Molecular and clinical characteristics of posterior fossa glioblastoma
results.summary: Posterior fossa GBMs show similar molecular profiles to supratentorial GBMs including IDH-wildtype status, EGFR amplification, and TERT promoter mutations. MGMT methylation status predicts temozolomide response regardless of tumor location. Young adult patients may harbor H3K27M mutations warranting testing.

results.title: Surgical management of cerebellar glioblastoma: extent of resection and outcomes
results.summary: Gross total resection is associated with improved survival. Suboccipital craniotomy is the standard approach. Key surgical risks include brainstem injury, cerebellar mutism, and CSF leak. Adjuvant treatment follows the Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide/radiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide).

output.summary: Cerebellar glioblastoma is rare (0.4-3.4% of all GBMs) and most commonly presents in middle-aged adults, though cases in young adults are documented. Standard treatment follows the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent temozolomide and radiation, then adjuvant temozolomide. MGMT promoter methylation predicts temozolomide response. Gross total resection correlates with improved survival. Unique posterior fossa considerations include risk of obstructive hydrocephalus, brainstem compression, and cerebellar mutism syndrome post-operatively.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 every 28 days
interactions: Combined oral contraceptives: Temozolomide may reduce efficacy of hormonal contraception due to potential enzyme interactions. Recommend additional barrier contraception.
Ferrous sulfate: No significant interaction, but separate administration by at least 2 hours from other medications to maximize absorption.
contraindications: Severe hepatic or renal impairment (current labs show normal function)
Pregnancy — temozolomide is Category D; effective contraception required
warnings: Myelosuppression: Monitor CBC weekly during concurrent phase, prior to each cycle during adjuvant phase. Absolute neutrophil count must be >1.5 x10^9/L and platelets >100 x10^9/L before each cycle.

Monitor for lymphopenia — PCP prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole recommended during concurrent phase. NOTE: Patient has sulfonamide allergy — use pentamidine inhalation or dapsone as alternative.
Hepatotoxicity: Monitor liver function tests periodically
Pre-existing anemia should be addressed — optimize iron supplementation before initiating myelosuppressive chemotherapy
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative nitrosourea if temozolomide not tolerated
Bevacizumab — may be considered for recurrent disease
Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields/Optune) — approved for supratentorial GBM, limited data for posterior fossa

output.query: hemangioblastoma vs glioblastoma cerebellar imaging differentiation

results.title: Imaging differentiation of posterior fossa tumors in adults
results.summary: Hemangioblastomas typically present as well-circumscribed cystic lesions with a brightly enhancing mural nodule and are associated with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome. In contrast, GBMs show irregular enhancement with central necrosis, infiltrative borders, and intratumoral hemorrhage. Perfusion imaging shows elevated rCBV in the mural nodule of hemangioblastomas and in the enhancing rim of GBMs.

results.title: Posterior fossa tumors in young adults: a diagnostic challenge
results.summary: Key differentiating features: hemangioblastomas have distinct borders, cystic component with mural nodule, and flow voids. GBMs show infiltrative margins, heterogeneous enhancement with necrosis, and peritumoral edema with restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim. MR spectroscopy may help: hemangioblastomas lack the elevated Cho/NAA ratio seen in GBMs.

output.summary: Hemangioblastomas and cerebellar glioblastomas can both present as posterior fossa masses in young adults, but their imaging characteristics differ substantially. Hemangioblastomas are typically well-circumscribed, cystic with an enhancing mural nodule, and associated with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome. GBMs show irregular heterogeneous enhancement with central necrosis, infiltrative borders, and elevated Cho/NAA ratio on MR spectroscopy. The presence of intratumoral hemorrhage, irregular ring enhancement, and necrosis in this patient favors GBM over hemangioblastoma.

differential.key_distinguishing: Metastases typically have well-defined borders with more prominent surrounding edema compared to tumor size. No known primary malignancy. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative. Infiltrative margins on MRI argue against metastatic disease.

Posterior fossa location in a young patient is consistent, but hemangioblastomas typically present as cystic lesions with a well-circumscribed enhancing mural nodule rather than irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis. No features of von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.
Can arise from the fourth ventricle in young adults but typically shows more homogeneous enhancement, calcification, and plastic growth pattern extending through the foramina of Luschka/Magendie.
More common in children. Typically arises from the vermis with homogeneous enhancement and diffusion restriction throughout the solid component, not peripheral ring enhancement with necrosis.

Ring enhancement can mimic GBM but abscesses show smooth thin-walled ring enhancement, restricted diffusion centrally (pus), and typically present with fever, elevated WBC, and infectious symptoms. Patient is afebrile with normal WBC.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with and without contrast to characterize the posterior fossa mass lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Heterogeneously enhancing masses in the right cerebellar hemisphere and vermis with central necrosis, intratumoral hemorrhage, and obstructive hydrocephalus

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity and assess cortical dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant intermittent slowing consistent with posterior fossa structural pathology

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, hepatic panel as pre-surgical baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild microcytic anemia consistent with iron deficiency; normal coagulation and organ function for surgical clearance

optimal_actions.action: Request MRI perfusion imaging to assess vascularity and support differentiation between high-grade glioma, metastasis, and other posterior fossa masses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim favoring high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy for metabolic characterization of the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to rule out primary extracranial malignancy (metastatic workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of extracranial primary malignancy, supporting primary CNS tumor

optimal_actions.action: Neurosurgical consultation for tissue diagnosis and management of obstructive hydrocephalus
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical plan for suboccipital craniotomy and tumor resection with possible external ventricular drain placement

optimal_actions.action: Obtain tissue diagnosis through stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection with molecular testing (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, EGFR, TERT)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO Grade 4 with MGMT promoter methylation

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for planned temozolomide chemotherapy given current medications and sulfonamide allergy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sulfonamide allergy requires alternative PCP prophylaxis; temozolomide interaction with OCP noted

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on cerebellar glioblastoma management and prognosis in young adults
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard Stupp protocol applies; posterior fossa location has unique surgical considerations

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the posterior fossa mass — CT alone is insufficient for surgical planning and differentiation of posterior fossa tumors
Urgently address obstructive hydrocephalus — neurosurgical consultation for possible external ventricular drain or ventriculostomy before definitive tumor surgery
Obtain tissue diagnosis via biopsy or resection — do not treat empirically based on imaging alone
Perform metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to rule out extracranial primary malignancy before assuming primary CNS tumor
Request molecular testing including IDH status, MGMT promoter methylation, and 1p/19q codeletion on tissue — these guide treatment decisions and prognosis
Note the sulfonamide allergy when planning PCP prophylaxis during temozolomide treatment — use alternative agents such as pentamidine or dapsone
contraindicated_actions: Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of obstructive hydrocephalus and posterior fossa mass effect — risk of transtentorial or tonsillar herniation
Empirically treating with high-dose corticosteroids before imaging — could obscure diagnosis if CNS lymphoma is in the differential
Delaying neurosurgical consultation in the presence of obstructive hydrocephalus and signs of elevated intracranial pressure
Administering trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PCP prophylaxis given the documented sulfonamide allergy
Initiating temozolomide without confirming histopathological diagnosis and molecular markers
Ignoring the pre-existing iron deficiency anemia before myelosuppressive chemotherapy
key_reasoning_points: Posterior fossa masses in young adults require a broad differential including hemangioblastoma, medulloblastoma, ependymoma, pilocytic astrocytoma, and metastases — cerebellar GBM is rare but must be considered when imaging shows infiltrative margins with heterogeneous enhancement and necrosis

The irregular contours, heterogeneous ring enhancement with central necrosis, and intratumoral hemorrhage on MRI are characteristic of GBM and help distinguish it from hemangioblastoma (cystic with mural nodule) and metastases (well-defined borders with disproportionate edema)
Obstructive hydrocephalus from fourth ventricle compression is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent management before definitive tumor surgery

Negative metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) supports a primary CNS neoplasm rather than cerebellar metastasis, despite the family history of breast and ovarian cancer which may raise clinical suspicion for metastatic disease

Family history of breast and ovarian cancer (maternal side) is a red herring for the brain lesion diagnosis but should prompt genetic counseling for BRCA testing independent of the current neurological presentation
MGMT promoter methylation status is critical for treatment planning — methylated MGMT predicts better response to temozolomide, which is part of the standard Stupp protocol for GBM

The sulfonamide allergy is clinically significant because standard PCP prophylaxis during temozolomide treatment uses trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole — the agent must recognize this and recommend alternatives

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to atypical patient age (young adult) for GBM, posterior fossa location which is uncommon for GBM, family history of breast/ovarian cancer acting as a red herring for metastatic disease, and imaging differential that includes hemangioblastoma and other posterior fossa tumors. The diagnosis requires integrating imaging characteristics with clinical context and ruling out alternative etiologies.
